title: Venture Roundtable: SpaceX IPO, AI's PR Crisis, and the Defense Tech Bubble | E2270

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right everybody, welcome back to TWIST. It is April 1st, 2026. This is not an April Fool's joke. Delian is back on the program. It's going to be based folks, with me my co-host and who will be the world's second greatest moderator today, Alex Wilhelm. Alex, let's get started here. But before we do, I always like to applaud, applaud. And I take my Plaud Pin, oh you use it on the wrist, and I press the button. Here we go. Delian, I'm now recording everything and I have all my action items. Action item: buy secondary, buy more secondary in Delian's company. What is Varda trading for on the secondary market? I need to increase my ownership.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: 20.50 a share, baby.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: 20.50 a share. Okay, I'm coming in at 19.25. I'm coming in at 19.25 with my bid. Plaud, I applaud you. Is there an offer?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: There is an offer. If you go to plaud.ai/twist, P-L-A-U-D.ai/twist, use the code twist, save 10% and then you won't forget everything you're supposed to do. And then Jason won't send you late night Slacks asking where the thing is that you promised him you'd do, which everyone absolutely loves.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well you took the job Alex. It was your choice to come work for me.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Absolutely, everyone gets to make a mistake in their life, I've made several. You know, here we are. We also have more guests than just Delian from Founders Fund and Varda. We also have Sal Churi. He is the man over at Trust Ventures, general partner there. Think regulated industries, companies like Base Power, Jason, firms that we absolutely love. Sal, thanks for being here.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Sal, I love Base Power. Mm.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Best company.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Mm, yum yum on that one. Just tell us in one sentence or two why you made that bet.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Grid's broken, we need to fix it. You look at US versus China energy generation, they've 10x-ed in the past 25 years, we're flat. If we don't fix it, we are in big trouble. Everything we want to do requires power. So Base has taken a pretty novel approach to solving that problem. They're putting batteries on people's homes. So you get a battery to back up your house instead of spending, you know, 40, 50,000 bucks on a Tesla Powerwall or a generator, you get a battery for 500 bucks, which is more like the cost of a plane ticket. Base puts a battery on your house, they back you up when the grid goes down. But lots of benefits to the grid and then Base has basically a distributed arbitrage machine sitting at your house. So all day it's taking all those intermittent sort of solar, wind, all these incredibly cheap renewables that are amazing, we just don't use them, and using them. So poles and wires move energy through space, batteries move energy through time. So Base is just carpet bombing the grid with batteries to lower energy costs and kind of help us use some of those renewables that today are really cheap but mostly underutilized.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So genius. Love the product, love the entire concept.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: If you want more about Base Power, we did have the founder on episode 2190 from October 2025. And Jason, please...

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Joined by yet another guest, we have Larson Jensen from Harpoon Ventures, former SEAL, now backing companies like Robust Intelligence and Merlin Labs. Larson, glad you're here.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Happy to be here guys, thanks for having me.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: All right, so the thing that happened right before we jumped on air everybody, is that Bloomberg reported that SpaceX has filed confidentially to go public. This means they're targeting probably a June IPO. Lots to come on this story, Jason, but finally we're here, we're making steps towards what could be the biggest IPO of all time. No inside information, but were you surprised by the timing?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Uh, no. I have—I don't want to say I have inside information, but everybody in the Valley is buzzing about this. There's been tons of secondary and all kinds of transactions for the last three years where people were either paring their position or building their position in the name. And I think the bigger, most interesting part of this is Elon Inc. Again, no inside information. I've met Elon a couple of times. Uh, it's quite possible that this is the flop and then the turn and the river to come. And if this is the flop and the turn and the river to come, the next discussion is going to be merging with Tesla, and then you're going to be able to buy the stock dollar sign ELON and maybe Boring Company, Neuralink come in next, and you can just buy a ten trillion dollar company that's solving the hardest problems on the planet led by—let's call it what it is—Elon is the greatest entrepreneur of this generation and, you know, top easily top five of all time. Some people would argue number one, two, three, and I'll leave it up to other people to make that decision, but certainly he's got 20 years left in him and the best is probably yet to come.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: So, 1.75 trillion theoretically is what the company's targeting. We're also going to possibly see OpenAI and Anthropic list this year. So we could have essentially three, you know, several hundred billion or trillion dollar IPOs this year. Is this a net positive for startup exits or is this an entirely different conversation than what we talk about when we discuss smaller company IPOs?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I mean look, it's sort of like testing the limits of the term startup, right? The private markets have—have really developed over the past couple of years and—and is able to support a company like SpaceX to this point is kind of wild. When it lists it'll be one of the largest companies in the world and, as Jason points out, you might end up getting a company that is kind of an amalgam of all of Elon's industries, which looks almost like a parallel economy in itself. It's kind of an insane—it's an insane thing. I think it's going to be great. I mean there's so much—there's so much liquidity tied up in this company. There's so many people who are so convicted on it. Uh, but I think, you know, it'll be one of the greatest wealth creation events in history. Uh, you think about folks who live in Austin, we're all like, 'Oh my goodness, buy a house now because it's about to get ugly.' Uh, but I think, you know, the same is true for all the folks who have been invested in SpaceX for, you know, 20 years, and I think the best is probably yet to come.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: for, you know, a decade now and, you know, even more recent folks, like we were investors in xAI. I think we're all looking at this and, and sort of thinking about the, the flop in the river, as Jason says.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Alright, so Delian, you're the Founders Fund kid on the show today. If you go to the portfolio page, the first entry, the first thing is SpaceX. So, tell us what the firm is thinking, what are you shooting for, and what will your return be on a net IRR basis from your SpaceX checks?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Ha. Yeah. I mean I think it's probably going to be one of the greatest, you know, sort of venture investments of all time. Yeah, I think we originally got in, you know, around like 2009 or so. We were one of the, you know, sort of first big, you know, sort of external checks and then along the way, you know, we've sort of continued to, you know, double down across a variety of our funds including some of our, you know, sort of latest growth funds. Um, yeah. I mean, what are we expecting? Look, I think, you know, SpaceX has basically built up, you know, the best, you know, sort of telecommunications business, you know, sort of ever, you know, sort of made, you know, through Starlink and they're obviously using, you know, sort of that advantage to tackle some even broader markets. They also are, you know, probably only people that have the ability to really take us to like the Moon and Mars in a super meaningful way, so who knows what the like sort of uncapped, you know, sort of potential upside of that is. Um, let's see. What are we, you know, sort of expecting? Yeah, it's obviously going to be, you know, sort of huge, you know, sort of wealth creation for basically, you know, all our early, you know, sort of LPs. Um, and, you know, I think we've also proven that, uh, we're willing to, you know, sort of have extremely long hold periods. I think, you know, from our very, you know, sort of first, uh, you know, sort of investment today, you know, it's been something like, you know, 18 years of hold. And so we are happy to, you know, sort of be very patient capital.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Larson, you guys are in Astranis, so you're in the satellite game. Do you think that there's going to be real competition on the space internet question in the next couple of years? 'Cause I know Amazon's getting involved, China has the thing going on. I'm just curious if you think that they're going to run away with it.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: They might run away with it, but I also think there's a lot of form factors that are going to make a lot of sense here. Like, I think one thing that Astranis is doing that SpaceX isn't to date, at least, is servicing, you know, specific countries, specific companies. And so if you want your own dedicated broadband access, you can get that through Astranis and not necessarily through SpaceX. But SpaceX is certainly much larger, especially right now. And I think there's a lot of other things that are really exciting about SpaceX. So, obviously LP liquidity is something that's been top of mind for every single LP across the board now, given how many of them have, you know, interests in SpaceX right now, priming the pump, getting some liquidity back to LPs to redeploy it into the venture ecosystem, I think is going to be a net positive. Also, when you think about the early employees of SpaceX, the every early founder I think can be more aggressive if they have some cushion in their bank account. They can buy a house, they can do these things. They're not pressured to exit early. So, founders that we've backed that are former SpaceX or the new ones that are going to be starting new companies, I think this is a net positive for them to be more risk seeking. And I also think that by virtue of SpaceX going to retail now, we have the opportunity for young kids to have access to this and they're sort of like, you know, in their brokerage accounts, effectively. Like, we have our kids in these brokerage accounts, my kids who are nine and seven don't stop asking about when they can buy SpaceX. And I think this is a net positive for the future generation.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: to also be a part of the most ambitious country or excuse me company in probably in world history and all the things that Elon's building. So I think there's a lot of positives that are coming from this IPO that are more than just meets the eye. Super happy for the Founders Fund guys, you guys are the goat, you guys crush it, but I also think there's a lot of other beneficiaries out there in the ecosystem that could lead for entrepreneurship to be even better in the decades ahead because of SpaceX.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Jason, you're always talking about, you know, getting more people to start companies and so forth. What do you think the liquidity effect will be on founder formation, if you will, in the next, I don't know, couple years?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I've been doing this job for decades now. Not a lot of people have worked with more startups than me. Over 600 investments. But honestly, Every.io might be the exception because Every.io is working with every new startup. It's the all-in-one finance, banking, and HR app. They understand startups and they're now at over 1,000 startups. And that means first-time founders working on their seed round, but also veteran teams who've raised a Series A or beyond. They can partner with Every and they are. And why are they partnering with them? Just to scale up their business, build out their teams, take care of things that are not core to your product or your service. That means banking, payroll, benefits, taxes, compliance. Everything you need to run your company is done in one place by one trusted partner. And they're going to give you 3% cashback on all company purchases sent directly to your bank account. So it's time to visit Every.io and get back to focusing on what really matters for your business. Every.io is going to help you do that. E-V-E-R-Y dot I-O.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Well, you know, one of the great things about America is the founders are so based and all bought in when it comes to entrepreneurship that the first thing founders do, much to the chagrin of their wealth managers, who are dweebs and dorks who get paid 1.X% of your 4 or 5% net after taxes returns, they try to stop them from investing in other startups. But that's kind of the reason why America is so great is because entrepreneurs, the second they get any money in their pocket, they're like, 'how can I blow this money? How can I 10x it? How can I 1000x it? Let's go to Vegas. Maybe I'll buy a car.' For me, it was buying like, you know, a Corvette and a one-touch espresso machine when I sold Weblogs Inc in 2005. Like two big, you know, $70,000 Corvette and a $3,000 espresso machine were my big purchases. But I was then like, 'hey, I wonder if I can angel invest in companies.' Now you take other countries like Europe slash Epcot Center, and when a family gets money in Epcot Center, Italy, France, all they do is try to protect it. 'I have to protect this, I have to protect this. We have to buy castles and build moats.' And they never recirculate that money. The reason why Australia is doing particularly well is because the founders of Atlassian and the founders of Canva also started venture firms and started to recirculate that. That's what I suspect will happen here.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you'll have a bunch of people retire from venture capital. Brian Singerman led Founders Fund for a bunch and I think he gave over the reins. So you'll have people who are impacted, I think, I'm sure the SpaceX thing probably had something to do with that. You know, they'll call in rich or whatever. But then they'll be like, you know what, Doug Leone came back to Sequoia yesterday and it was like the day before April Fool's and I'm like... I was always saying Doug is retired, but every time I go to the office and hang out with the Sequoia boys and gals, they... Doug's there walking around. So I don't understand like this retirement. I think it's going to be a massive amount of recirculation and I think it's going to save, you know, these IPOs. If Stripe goes out, Discord goes out, SpaceX, and then these two wildcards, I, you know, OpenAI and Anthropic. This is going to reinvigorate venture capital, which under Biden's four years of the wrath of Lina Khan was considered the worst asset class. Now we're going to go back to it being the best asset class and everybody's going to want a slice after the last five years of 'this is a complete utter disaster, what fool would ever put their money into venture capital?' So if you're going to be in the startup game, you need to have like actual true resilience and man, I gotta tell you, that was... that was trying for the four years during Lina Khan to be like 'is this even worth it?' I heard so many VCs, 'is it worth it to be in this? Like none of these companies are ever going to go public, none can ever merge.' And here we are. It's happening folks. Venture capital is back on the menu.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: That kind of brings us into the first thing I wanted to dig into today, which is the difference between how we talk about AI and technology today here on the show and in similar places in and around the tech scene and some public polling data that just dropped. I'm a little bit worried that what we're seeing is a lot of technologists building the future and a lot of the public being kind of left behind in a sense, concerned, worried about job loss. There's a Quinnipiac poll that shows you know Americans are using more and more AI, but numbers on trust and so forth are going down. Now Sal, you wrote a book called The Trust Revolution literally all about this type of thing. I'm curious, one, how big is the gap between the public and the technology space today and is it a bridgeable chasm before something bad happens at the election box that leads to onerous regulation crimping everyone's fun?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I spend all day helping solve regulatory complexity. I'm a recovering law professor, this is like what I focus on all day. I think there's a couple things to be thinking about with respect to AI, data centers. 80% of people in both parties, this is not a partisan thing, are skeptical of or negative on AI and/or data centers, right? If you look at the first industrial revolution as an analog, like, you know, they were... you had the Pinkertons firing shots alongside Frick at, you know, Carnegie steel production facilities. So we've got trillions of dollars in CAPEX sitting...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: behind chain-link fences effectively undefended? Like, you know, it's a real concern. And then there's another dynamic where you've got folks like Sam and Dario, like they're out there raising like billions and billions of dollars by saying we're just going to replace people and, you know, in many ways it doesn't make them the best spokespeople because the average person hears that and actually thinks things are much further along than they are. And then you've got some CEOs who are actually starting to let people go and using AI as an excuse. Now, I actually don't even think that that's really so true yet, but it will become true. And so you're already seeing this kind of preemptive backlash. Now, at the same time you've got things like robotics that are really starting to take off, the embodied AI stuff, one of the companies that we're most excited about is Physical Intelligence. It's a visual language action model, so it's sort of bringing that LLM concept into the physical world can, you know, pilot a drone, can run a surgery robot, can run an autonomous vehicle. These things are starting to reach into the world of atoms and not just the world of bits and I think it's going to start touching a lot more people. So I think we're headed into the World War III of regulatory fights, we've already kind of engaged on a lot of these across the states, but there's going to be significant political backlash to this.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: There's this guy named Jason. And uh, he recently had a tweet all about how we've reached AGI, millions of jobs are being lost as we speak, retirement of entire careers, people getting kind of locked out of the economy. Uh, Jason, are you to blame for the public sentiment crisis here?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No. Putting myself aside, I have my own feelings, but our industry has a terrible communication problem. You know, you have on one side Dario who is like, it's over, all jobs are over, AGI's almost here, Skynet's going to be revealed, we're blowing everything up. And then you have on the other side Jensen, who is like, don't worry, every cab driver's going to be a chauffeur and just carry your bags, which is also kind of a little bit too lovey-dovey. Obviously, you know, there's no drivers in Waymos and his position was the other week, yeah, you'll still have a driver in a Waymo, they'll be serving you coffee or whatever. Like, that's not happening. You know who's done a great job? Michael Dell. You know why Michael Dell's done a great job is because he put $6 billion of his own money, which is a fraction of his net worth. Forget about the stupid giving pledge. If you just say to Americans, hey, we're going to get the other 40% of people who are not participating in the equity holding of these great companies, if you just change that, everybody's going to feel differently. And you know how you know this? Anybody who's on this call who has exposure to a venture firm in SpaceX, in Claude, or any of these companies, we feel great about the economy. Any of our family members who have exposure to our venture firms or, you know, who have a 401k with Google in it or Nvidia in it, they feel great. People who don't feel great? They don't own equities. They don't own a piece of the prize. What we really need to do in my mind is be honest with people. There are going to be entire jobs retired just like phone operators and receptionists are gone. The typing pool is gone. The mailroom is gone.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Society went on. It's not a big deal. Bad jobs that can be automated always get retired, and it may happen a little faster this time. At the same time, by the way, all of these great entrepreneurs, all of these great philanthropists, instead of putting money into nonprofits, which take 60 cents on the dollar, and they pay these, you know, overpriced mids to, you know, be in $110 office space, which is better than all of our collective office space, and they sit there and, you know, yente it up and, you know, decide they're going to try to save the world while 70 cents on the dollar goes to their comp and their office space, just give people directly some goddamn shares of SpaceX and Anthropic and the entire problem is solved. And then America will be unstoppable. Every American has $10,000 in the market, they're going to look at it totally different. They're going to watch their net worth go up. So, I would say we should start a new giving pledge, and it should be based on Michael Dell's Trump accounts or America... what were they called? What were they called before Trump accounts?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Save America or something like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Invest America. Invest America.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Invest America. Here's an idea.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's easier than ever to build a great new product and launching a startup even as a solopreneur is getting easier and easier. But as my experienced founders already know, there's a lot more to starting a company than just putting up a website or even building a product. If you're serious about going into business, you need a Delaware C Corp. That's going to give you a serious leg up on your competition and you can be taken seriously by investors. That's where Northwest Registered Agent comes in. Just a few clicks, they're going to give you the perfect start to your new enterprise: a real identity, a domain, a custom website, a business email, all the public filings done, and even a phone number. And you'll complete this process in under ten minutes. Plus, you're going to get all sorts of free tools and resources from step-by-step guides, compliance reminders, and an online account that's going to keep everything organized, even as your business grows. So get all the advantages of a Delaware C Corp regardless of where in the US you're operating out of. northwestregisteredagent.com/twist.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Everybody on... everybody working in Silicon Valley gives 5% of their shares that they make, they get some great tax benefit and they give it to Invest America. If you told me, hey, J-Cal, will you pledge 5% of all the Uber and Robinhood shares to Invest America? I'd be like, sure. If it keeps us from having a revolution and socialism, why not? Make it opt-in and see if you can get 1,000 shareholders in these great companies to join it. Solves the entire problem. Because then if there is job displacement and somebody needs to take two years off, they can tap that Invest America account if they're destitute and need to be retrained, or at least they're not going to be scared about retirement. Anyway, that's my TED talk. Thanks for coming.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I want to get Delian in here because Founders Fund is relatively outspoken. I was just reading Trace's post in the Free Press, sorry, about industrialization and the need for public-private partnerships and so forth. Do you guys think about the political issues regarding AI and...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Gentlemen, because I think if anyone would have a strident opinion, it might be Founders Fund.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Think that, you know, we obviously come from a, you know, sort of more, you know, sort of libertarian, you know sort of point of view where it's, you know, sort of, let technology accelerate and, you know, sort of deal with the societal implications, you know, sort of later, but there was this, you know, sort of famous email between Peter and Zuckerberg that, you know, sort of got leaked out, from back in, you know, sort of 2020, that basically articulated this point of, you know, uh, if, you know, more of the, you know, sort of population doesn't get to, uh, you know, participate in the, you know, sort of growing, you know, sort of economy, you are going to start to see this type of more, you know, sort of socialist, you know, sort of revolution. And it was a discussion about that, plus, you know, sort of Zuckerberg being one of the more prominent millennials, in his generation, to, you know, try and, you know, sort of figure out how to, you know, sort of fight back against it.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Last week I got to be, you know, sort of on stage at the, uh, Hill and Valley Forum, which is this, you know, sort of event I've been organizing for a couple years, and we do our best to, you know, sort of highlight, you know, sort of both sides of the aisle. And so I'll highlight an argument that like, yeah, I'm not saying I necessarily agree with, but was kind of interesting to, you know, sort of hear from, which is, uh, from, uh, this panel with Vinod Khosla and Senator Cantwell. Vinod's, you know, sort of idea was, you know, as a mechanism to, you know, start to, you know, sort of lift up the, you know, uh, yeah, yeah, bottom of the, you know, sort of economic, you know, sort of totem pole, um, was to, you know, basically make it so there's a singular tax that is the equivalent tax rate for whether it's capital gains, you know, basically or income. And if you do that, you actually, you know, can then eliminate the, you know, bottom 125 million from the tax, you know, sort of basis, anybody that basically makes anything below 100-125k.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: It's so, I do think you'd, you'd need to start to think about how do you make it so that, you know, with these like AI productivity gains, you have a much broader, you know, sort of portion of the population that gets to, you know, participate in this upside. Some of it may come from, you know, things like Invest America where you're getting to participate in the upside. Some of it is remove some of the economic drag of, you know, sort of the lower part of the, you know, sort of population, e.g., you know, sort of taxes.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, just really quickly. Here's the email in question and, uh, Thiel wrote, 'When one has too much student debt or if housing is too unaffordable, then one will have negative capital for a long time, find it hard to start accumulating capital, one form of real estate, and if no one has a stake in the capitalist system, then one may well turn against it.' I guess we're operationalizing that, Jason, in term, to put it into, into practice. So, Larson, are you gonna follow Jason and put 5% of your net worth into other people's accounts so they don't vote for...

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: You know, I actually, you know, I'll disagree with Jason on some of this, and I, I think that this is actually coming. I, I don't live in Silicon Valley, I don't think many people here actually do on the call here today, but some of my friends that I go and see at my kid's birthday parties, like, own car dealerships. And they're the ones telling me that they're using Claude code and they're replacing workers at their own car dealership. And so I think it's actually happening. And I think the other thing, it's one thing to sort of redistribute wealth through whatever the mechanism is, like universal basic income, some of this like letting people participate is one thing. But I think, you know, most of us here and I think most people in life put a lot of their identity behind what they do. And it's easy for Silicon Valley to trivialize that, you know, some jobs aren't important and you get to go do something else, but these jobs are important to those people. And so I think we're walking our way

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right into a bear trap and part of the reason why Trump got elected is because he wanted to reshore jobs that are offshore to China. And so China became the boogeyman and I think rightfully so. And a large part of his base supported Trump because of that. What I'm concerned about now is that the tech industry is walking to the same trap again that is a Bernie narrative, is an AOC narrative, is an Elizabeth Warren narrative, and tech has aligned itself, like it or not, with the Trump administration. And so here we are where we're going to walk ourselves into a trap I think again that if we don't do things right and we don't actually think through the consequences of this as a society, we could walk ourselves into a position where AI is taking jobs the same way that China took our jobs and we have a huge disenfranchised worker base that isn't like upset about the fact that they're doing a job, isn't upset the fact that they're being a cabby and now all of a sudden they get to carry luggage. They might like it, they're providing for their family. Like I can't speak for them, but I think everybody that has a job that is providing for their families takes some at least on being a male, you take some pride in that and you want to do that. And so I think we're walking our way into that sort of like blindly. And I think it's just too, too, too simple for Silicon Valley to say hey, we're going to pay you and everything's going to be fine. I don't think that's going to happen.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: To be clear, Larson, I am with you, I am not a fan of UBI because it takes away purpose. But I am for taking a look at the disparity in wealth, which is incredibly real and is going to get worse.

full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think like sometimes I like don't totally, you know, sort of buy that argument where I think people like think that the reason that like, you know, sort of Shanghai is such a great place to, like, you know, do consumer electronics is like because of state funding. It's like, no, at this point they've just built by far the best ecosystem for that type of talent, for that type of, you know, sort of production. I think like, you know, in in relation to, you know, sort of bio, um, you know, sort of the best, youell, you know, sort of attempt at this in the past couple years was like the ABC, you know, sort of incubation Resilience, right, which was trying to like, you know, reshore and create the like Wuxi Biologics, Wuxi Small Molecules, which is like a generic, you know, sort of drug producer in China and bring that, you know, basically back to, you know, back to America. Um, but you know, I think a lot of why they like, you know, ultimately, you know, sort of didn't end up, you know, sort of working out the way they wanted was like, they didn't really figure out how to pair, you know, sort of public and private dollars. But again, there was no like, you know, actually studying China and looking at like, you know, how do you actually get ahead of that? Like a bit of why Wuxi, you know, sort of works in China is like they just have infinite low-cost labor, you know, basically chemists.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think there's just some things here that are uneconomical and private venture capital or PE or private debt markets are just not going to finance. And I think that's where Uncle Sam needs to step in.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: and say like this is imperative to our national prosperity and security, we need to invest in this because you know, it's sort of like you know, during TARP buyer of last resort thing, whether or not that was a good policy, I think we have a similar type of thing that's happening right now. We need to be a funder of you know, last resort to get things on step, to validate the unit economics, and then let the private markets take over.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: What mechanism would you use for that? And what are some target areas to Jason's point, where you think that would apply?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I go back to the pharmaceutical industry, I think is a great one. I think it's like not talked about enough. I think it's extremely important to the health and of our entire populace. We went through a pandemic, all of these things are really important, and I think that's something that we need to you know, invigorate here. Obviously you know, semis and TSMC and all that stuff is is already talked about a lot and I think that we're making good progress towards that, but I think you know, thinking about the health and security of our population, that's one that's not talked about enough.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Jason, who should lead the nation in getting that done, do you think? Should that be governors, or do you think that's a federal question?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: If only we had a business focused you know, chief executive in the White House who wasn't starting endless foreign wars, um and who was a great negotiator and had all the technology community and business community willing to come to the White House anytime he snapped his fingers. We have like half of that right now. Unfortunately, the entire administration has been distracted by a forever war. If that ends, like Trump is on his way to doing this, you know, and it's I think Michael Bloomberg, Trump, you know, my uh you know, Jamie Dimon, Eisner, pick your CEO, Schultz, CEO, former CEO who becomes president, who can explain this to politicians in a very deft way and explain to the American public that this is not grift. That's really the only reason why people get upset about this is they think oh, they're just giving Intel 10 billion dollars because Intel's a fuck up and they can't make a real business, so they're giving they're fucking maxing tax dollars to the most fucking maxed company. When you really just need to make small incentives. Remove, obviously removing um you know, the regulations, great. And then this is what I liked about the loan program is people opted into it, they paid it back, and if you just put a little warrant coverage in there, then it makes up for all the losses. I'm not saying it should be like the purpose of our government, but in the case of we don't have pharmaceuticals, you want a private public partnership. You don't want the government doing anything except maybe protecting citizens. You know, they they suck at whatever they do. Look at the education system, look at our health care system. We pay more than any we pay two or three times any other country for health care and for education, and we get half the results.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: When people propose a government policy, they're always imagining like a philosopher king administering it, but actually you get the DMV. Like that's the important thing to remember.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: This is why Singapore is so interesting to me. Have you guys been to Singapore before? One? You have.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: It's worth going and just in that country, the way it was set up by Lee Kuan—

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yew.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Yew. Lee Kuan Yew, who is like, that's my government warrior CEO, like war mode CEO. If you were the equivalent of a Harvard MBA, you came out of Stanford, you go work for the government there. You get paid twice the private sector. It's literally the elites, the elite performers, not elites like in the way we use it here in America. The most elite performers consider working for the government this incredible thing. It's kind of like the Navy SEALs, Lon, where it's like, you say you're a Navy SEAL, we're all like, whoa.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: That's legit. We need all of government to have that. You need to have like, you know, 80% of the people that try out for any role in government don't make it because you're not good enough. And like, there needs to be that level of prestige and like those types of people that are in the seat and, you know, it's—

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: As opposed to government being the place where people who, you know, can't cut it, can't get another job, yeah. And that's the turnaround we need. I mean, this is why you can say what you want about DOGE, but, or just making the government smaller. If we're sitting here and our industry is like, you know what? We can do this with 10% less people, like our government for the last 50 years under both all types of government, Republicans, Democrats and everybody in between, moderates, etc., has been like, you know what the solution here is? Let's build the size of the government. Like, that's never the solution. More of adding more to what's not working is almost never the solution. It's like hitting the accelerator while you're spinning, you know, on ice coming out of a turn, you're like—

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: That's not what you do.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: That's not what you do. Just hold the steering wheel straight and just ride it out. Like to just adding more speed never helps a rocket that's off course, right, Lon?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: No. And then, you know, so speaking of, we are now, you know, sort of L-minus exactly four hours to when, you know, humans go back to the moon for the first time in 50 years.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I'm so excited. Tell us about this, Delian. What is the story here? Why is this happening? Why is our government doing it? What exactly, they're going to go around the moon for 10 days with humans on the top of this Artemis rocket. Like, that's what I know. Why? And what are we going to prove by doing this?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I thought there's this really phenomenal article—I don't know if it makes sense to, you know, sort of bring up, written by this guy, Eric Berger, last night at Ars Technica, talking about like why for the first time he's excited about, you know, sort of this mission versus he hasn't been for the past couple Artemis's. I would say post-like the space shuttle era, right? Which you got, you know, sort of shut down, you know, during the Obama era. NASA just got really lost. Like, you know...

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: It was just like a very confusing architecture that they were saying for coming back like the moon and Mars. They were talking about basically putting like a space station in low Earth orbit, sorry, in moon orbit as like a way station on the way down to the surface of the moon. But they didn't really have a plan to get down to the surface, so we're going to put a bunch of astronauts orbiting the moon, but what's the point? Because like the whole point is like the moon has resources, you can mine it, you can build things there, but like if you're in orbit around it, you can't sort of do that. And he was like, this time it finally feels different. And a part of it, like Jared has just done some phenomenal work in the past, like I think he's been, you know, sort of, you know, nominated or confirmed for the past three months. He shut down all the nonsense, all the things that just felt very confusing and vague and had no clear plan at NASA for the past 15 years. He basically shut down. This is the third time that this, you know, sort of vehicle's launched, but it's the first time where it kind of makes sense in a technical tech tree, you know, sort of road map where we're basically repeating effectively the Apollo 9/10 missions, which were basically missions that like went to the moon and got close to the moon, but came back, but we're basically testing the system. And then we're basically doing that, you know, sort of same thing. Um, this vehicle has flown, you know, basically three times. The first was I want to say in 2014, and that was like a really, you know, low Earth orbit mission coming back. The second time was in 2022. There the, you know, sort of spacecraft went much further and higher up and we practiced basically reentering at a faster speed. And this time we're basically going around the moon. The change that he's also made is for the next mission, Artemis 3, what we're going to do is rather than going back to the moon again, we're actually going to practice docking with either Starship lunar lander or the Blue Origin lunar lander, which is, um, you know, a good practice test for eventually that docking basically happening by the moon. And then Artemis 4 would be ideally the first, you know, basically boots on the moon and that's currently planned for I believe, um, you know, sort of late 2028. I, you know, the, the rocket and the spacecraft honestly may or may not be the ones that we actually use to go back to the moon. So a bit of this is Jared sort of inheriting the, um, historical architecture, but what he has pushed is like, we are going to start launching this rocket way more frequently. We're going to be flying this spacecraft way more frequently. And I think that gives them the political air cover for like if in a year, 18 months, this rocket, which right now has flown like, you know, three times in the last 15 years, so not particularly high cadence, if they don't get to Jared's new cadence, they'll probably just shut that program and then pivot fully to some like Starship, Dragon, you know, Blue Origin, Blue Lander, etc. type of, you know, sort of architecture. And so, um, I think today is like super, super exciting. Um, you know, again, we'll go in very, very close to the moon and be, you know, doing a really good practice basically, you know, for a lunar landing run. Um, and it's furthest humans have been away from Earth in, you know, 50, 53 years. Um, but also I don't think we have like a super obvious like, here's how we're going to, you know, get back to the moon and, you know, put boots on it, you know, immediately.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but we're freaking trying and that makes me so happy. Like this entire Artemis thing, the fact that we're doing it just brings joy to my heart, Alex. I want to go to the moon myself. So the faster we go there, the faster I have a small chance. Jason, are you excited? You going to watch? Show it to the girls?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I mean, I'm... I guess I'm excited. I think I've become a bit numb to the excitement of launches since Elon has... is doing one every...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: 3 days and they're so predictable now, which I think says something. I mean what is the cadence right now of hopping on a Falcon? Like the Falcon is the one that goes up every 2 or 3 days now. What is the number? Falcon's like...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: 150, 160 per year.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So basically, basically it's like the train- it's like getting the B train or something. Like yeah you can- you can get the bus. full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm

full_transcript.text: Am I wrong? Close?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: It's very close.

full_transcript.text: It's very close.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: 66 years from Kitty Hawk to...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Okay, okay.

Like we- we just need to be a little more aggressive in terms of like or like raise the bar in terms of transportation. It's like it's- we're operating...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: optimizing for the cheapest possible ticket in the worst possible experience. Like, I just want to have inspiring transportation.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Well, after this conversation I'm pretty excited about the future in terms of compute and improving Americans' industrial capacity and going back to space. So I'm pretty optimistic Jason, I think we're making good progress. Don't be so, don't be so down.

full_transcript.text: No, I'm just— there's some things that feel like I need right now. full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Lon? Entire net worth has to be put in except for your primary residence, has to be put in Anthropic or OpenAI. 3, 2, go.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Dahlian, I don't know if you've got a bet here or...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: We're over a billion at Cost in both businesses, so I'm not allowed to say which is my favorite child.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, if your friend, uh, alien was asked this question, what would he say? Your friend alien.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I'm a little biased towards— let's say, okay, how about this? Pablo Esparohov, my little brother, who's the CTO of a really cool company in New York, definitely spends a lot more on Anthropic than he does OpenAI.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What is his— what's your baby bro's company? Give him a shout-out here.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm

And so they help out.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: out with that newly mandated process and get you paid more accurately and on time. Go Pablo.

highlights: SpaceX has reportedly filed for a confidential IPO targeting a June listing at a $1.75 trillion valuation, sparking speculation about the eventual merger of Elon Musk’s ventures into a single "Elon Inc" holding company.

To address public anxiety over AI-driven job loss, the panel proposed "Invest America," a model where a percentage of startup equity is redistributed to citizens to ensure the broader population benefits from the gains of automation.

Investors are increasingly favoring hardware and infrastructure companies because software-only moats are perceived as vulnerable in the AI era, leading to massive valuations for wearable tech, energy storage, and defense firms.

A debate over Marc Andreessen’s rejection of introspection highlighted a "violent execution" philosophy, where making rapid, aggressive decisions is considered more vital for elite entrepreneurs than ruminating on past mistakes.

Energy infrastructure is emerging as a top-tier investment sector, with startups like Base Power deploying residential batteries to act as a "distributed arbitrage machine" that stabilizes the grid and lowers renewable energy costs.
summary:
This episode of *TWIST*, set in April 2026, centers on the blockbuster news of SpaceX’s confidential IPO filing, with a targeted valuation of $1.75 trillion. The panel discusses the potential emergence of "Elon Inc," a theoretical merger of Musk’s various ventures into a single, ten-trillion-dollar "parallel economy." This massive liquidity event is framed as a turning point for venture capital, marking a shift away from the regulatory "wrath" of the early 2020s and toward a new era of "American Resilience." A major theme is the resurgence of hardware and infrastructure—ranging from Base Power’s distributed battery grid to defense tech and undersea mining—as investors increasingly view physical "atoms" as more defensible moats than AI-vulnerable software "bits."

The conversation also tackles the social and philosophical challenges of the AI age, specifically the "bridgeable chasm" between technologists and a public fearful of job loss. The hosts debate the "Invest America" concept, an idea to grant citizens equity in major tech companies to prevent a socialist backlash against wealth disparity. This leads into a critique of "therapy culture" versus the "execution-first" mindset of elite entrepreneurs. Referencing Marc Andreessen’s recent comments, the panel argues that high-level success requires a rejection of rumination in favor of "violently executed" plans. The episode concludes with a look at the Artemis missions and the goal of establishing a sovereign domestic supply chain for rare earths and pharmaceuticals to ensure American competitiveness against China.

Today’s show:
Silicon Valley just lined up its biggest liquidity event in years: SpaceX filed confidentially for an IPO that could target a $1.75 trillion valuation — potentially the largest public offering in history. Meanwhile, a new Quinnipiac poll shows that 62% of Americans aren't excited about AI, and 70% think it will destroy their jobs. The disconnect between Sand Hill Road and Main Street has never been wider, and this panel is here to argue about what to do about it.
Jason Calacanis and co-host Alex Wilhelm are joined by Delian Asparouhov, Partner at Founders Fund and co-founder of Varda Space Industries; Salen Churi, General Partner at Trust Ventures, focused on regulated industries and physical infrastructure; and Larsen Jensen, Founder and GP of Harpoon Ventures and former Navy SEAL.
They dig into the SpaceX IPO and what it means for LP liquidity, founder formation, and venture capital's comeback story; the gap between Silicon Valley's AI optimism and public hostility; whether defense tech is in a bubble after $49 billion flooded the sector in 2025; the Marc Andreessen "zero introspection" thesis for founders; America's reindustrialization challenge and the rare earths supply chain crisis; and Artemis’s path to putting boots back on the Moon.
